title: Maximum & minimum prices
summary:
## The big idea Sometimes a government looks at the price a free market produces and simply says *no*. Rents 'too high' for young workers, milk prices 'too low' for farmers to survive — so it passes a law fixing the price. These **price controls** are the bluntest tool in the kit, and the economics is beautifully predictable: hold a price away from equilibrium and the market pushes back with a **shortage** or a **surplus**. ## Maximum (ceiling) prices A **maximum price** is a legal *upper* limit — think rent controls in New York, capped bread or fuel prices in many developing countries. The aim is **affordability** of essentials. The golden rule: to have any effect the ceiling must be set **below the free-market equilibrium**. (A ceiling above equilibrium changes nothing — the market never gets there.) At the capped price, buyers want more than sellers will supply: **quantity demanded > quantity supplied**, a **shortage (excess demand)**.
**What each part means:** the free market would settle at **P\*** and **Q\***. The government caps the price at the orange line **Pmax**, *below* P\*. At that cheaper price buyers want **Qd** (read off the red demand curve) but sellers only offer **Qs** (read off the yellow supply curve). The horizontal arrow between Qs and Qd is the **shortage** — customers who want the good at the legal price and cannot get it. Only Qs units are actually traded — *fewer* than in the free market.
**Consequences of the shortage:** queues and waiting lists; sellers or officials must **ration** (first-come-first-served, coupons, favouritism); a **black market** appears where the good is illegally resold above the cap; and quality falls, because sellers have no incentive to maintain a good they can't price properly (a classic complaint about rent-controlled flats). To ease the shortage the government can try to shift **supply right** — subsidise producers or release buffer stocks. ## Minimum (floor) prices A **minimum price** is a legal *lower* limit, set to protect producers' incomes (farm price supports) or to discourage consumption (Scotland's minimum unit price for alcohol). The **national minimum wage** is the same diagram applied to the labour market. The mirror-image rule: it only bites if set **above equilibrium**. At the floor price, sellers offer more than buyers want: **quantity supplied > quantity demanded — a surplus (excess supply)**. Consequences: unsold stockpiles, government spending to buy up the surplus (the EU's old 'butter mountains'), wasted resources and over-production. ## Buffer stock schemes For volatile commodity markets, a **buffer stock scheme** tries to hold price inside a band: the agency **buys and stores** the commodity when price falls to the floor, and **sells from its stores** when price hits the ceiling. It can smooth farmer incomes and consumer prices — but it needs money and warehouses forever, only works for storable (non-perishable) goods, collapses if the price band is set wrong, and history is littered with failed schemes (tin, coffee). ## Evaluation Price controls treat the *symptom* (the price) rather than the *cause* (the underlying demand or supply). A rent cap doesn't build a single flat — the real problem is too little housing supply. Effectiveness depends on **how far the control is from equilibrium**, the **elasticities** (inelastic curves → smaller shortages/surpluses), and **enforcement**. Economists usually prefer subsidies, direct provision or cash transfers, which help the target group without banning mutually beneficial trades. Always weigh the equity gain against the efficiency loss — and the risk of **government failure**.
## ⚡ Quick revision - **Maximum price**: legal ceiling, bites only **below** P\* → **shortage** (Qd > Qs) → queues, rationing, black markets, quality decline. - **Minimum price**: legal floor, bites only **above** P\* → **surplus** (Qs > Qd) → stockpiles, government purchase costs. - Quantity actually traded = the **short side** of the market (Qs under a ceiling, Qd under a floor). - Size of shortage/surplus depends on **elasticities** of D and S. - **Buffer stock**: buy at the floor, sell at the ceiling — storage costs, needs storable goods, hard to set the band. - Evaluate: treats symptom not cause; alternatives = subsidies, provision, income support; government-failure risk.

keyTerms.definition: A legal upper limit on price, effective only if set below the equilibrium; causes shortages.
A legal lower limit on price, effective only if set above the equilibrium; causes surpluses.
When quantity demanded exceeds quantity supplied at the controlled price.
When quantity supplied exceeds quantity demanded at the controlled price.
An illegal market where goods are traded above a maximum price during a shortage.
A price-stabilisation scheme that buys surplus at a floor and sells stock at a ceiling.

diagrams: **Maximum price:** D/S diagram with a horizontal price line BELOW equilibrium; label the shortage as the horizontal distance between quantity demanded and quantity supplied.
**Minimum price:** D/S diagram with a horizontal price line ABOVE equilibrium; label the surplus (excess supply).
**Buffer stock:** show a target price band; buying adds to demand when price hits the floor, selling adds to supply when price hits the ceiling.
tips: State the golden rule: a maximum price only bites **below** equilibrium; a minimum price only bites **above** it. Getting this wrong loses the whole answer.
Always identify and **label the shortage or surplus** on the diagram, then explain the consequences (black markets, waste).
Bring elasticity in: the **size** of the shortage/surplus depends on the elasticities of demand and supply.
Evaluate with **alternatives** (subsidies, income support) and note the risk of **government failure**.
Use real examples: **New York City rent controls, Venezuela's price controls, the EU's farm price supports, Scotland's minimum alcohol price**, and national minimum wages.

channels.name: tutor2u Economics
channels.why: Clear max/min price diagrams and evaluation.

channels.name: EconplusDal
channels.why: Strong on price controls and buffer stocks.

revisionNotes:
# Maximum & minimum prices — quick revision

**Maximum (ceiling) price** — for affordability (rent controls, capped staples)
- Only effective **below** equilibrium.
- → **Shortage**: Qd > Qs at Pmax; only Qs traded (the short side).
- Consequences: queues, rationing, **black markets**, falling quality.
- Fixes: shift supply right (subsidies, buffer-stock releases) or ration fairly.

**Minimum (floor) price** — to protect producers / deter consumption (farm supports, minimum wage, alcohol pricing)
- Only effective **above** equilibrium.
- → **Surplus**: Qs > Qd; unsold stock, government may buy it up (cost!), over-production.

**Buffer stock scheme**
- Buy & store at the floor; sell stores at the ceiling → stabilises price inside a band.
- Problems: storage cost, perishables, wrong band, scheme can run out of money or stock.

**Evaluation core**
- Controls treat the **symptom** (price), not the cause (supply/demand).
- Size of distortion depends on **elasticities** and how far the control sits from P\*.
- Alternatives: subsidies, direct provision, cash transfers.
- Golden rule first, always: ceiling below / floor above equilibrium — then label the shortage or surplus on the diagram.
questions:
# Practice questions — Maximum & minimum prices **Original practice questions in the Cambridge 9708 style** (not copied from real papers). Attempt each, reveal the answer, then self-mark. Links to real past papers are in the Past-paper questions panel. ## Paper 1 style — multiple choice **1.** A maximum price will only affect a market if it is set: - A — above the equilibrium price - B — below the equilibrium price - C — equal to the equilibrium price - D — above the minimum price
▸ Show answer

B. A ceiling above equilibrium never binds — the market price is already lower. Only a ceiling below P* forces the price away from equilibrium and creates a shortage.

**2.** A government sets an effective minimum price for wheat. The most likely result is: - A — excess demand for wheat - B — a shortage of wheat - C — excess supply of wheat - D — a fall in wheat production
▸ Show answer

C. A binding floor sits above equilibrium: farmers supply more, buyers take less → a surplus (excess supply), which someone must store, buy or destroy.

**3.** Under an effective maximum price, the quantity actually traded is: - A — the quantity demanded at the maximum price - B — the quantity supplied at the maximum price - C — the equilibrium quantity - D — zero
▸ Show answer

B. You can't buy what nobody sells: trade is limited by the short side of the market — supply, under a ceiling. Note this is less than the free-market quantity.

**4.** In a buffer stock scheme, the agency buys the commodity when: - A — the market price rises to the ceiling of the band - B — the market price falls to the floor of the band - C — consumer demand is high - D — stocks are running low
▸ Show answer

B. Buying at the floor adds demand and stops the price falling further; selling from stores at the ceiling stops it rising further.

## Paper 2 style — data response > A fast-growing city caps rents on apartments at 20% below their market level. Two years later, a report finds: waiting lists for controlled flats; landlords converting apartments into offices or leaving them unmaintained; and a growing informal market where tenants illegally sublet at much higher rents. House-building has slowed. **(a)** Define 'maximum price'. **[2]** **(b)** Explain, with the aid of a diagram, why the rent cap has produced waiting lists. **[4]** **(c)** Analyse **two** likely effects of the rent cap on the quality and quantity of rental housing over time. **[6]** **(d)** Assess whether rent control is the best way to make housing affordable in this city. **[8]**
▸ Show mark guidance

(a) A legal upper limit on a price, set below equilibrium to make a good more affordable. [1+1]

(b) Diagram: Pmax below P*; at Pmax, Qd > Qs → shortage; waiting lists are rationing by time instead of price. [diagram 2, application 2]

(c) (i) Lower return → landlords cut maintenance → quality falls; (ii) supply shrinks over time (conversions, less building) → supply becomes more elastic long run, so the shortage worsens. Develop the chains. [3+3]

(d) For: immediate relief for existing tenants; politically fast. Against: shortage, informal markets, deters the new building that would actually cut rents; benefits the lucky insiders, not the poorest. Alternatives: planning reform/build more, housing benefit, subsidised construction. Judgement: cap treats the symptom; supply-side fixes tackle the cause. [8]

## Paper 2 / 4 style — essays **Essay 1 (a)** Explain, with the aid of a diagram, the effects of an effective minimum price on a market. **[8]** **(b)** Assess whether a minimum price is an effective way to reduce the consumption of alcohol. **[12]**
▸ Show mark guidance

(a) Floor above P*; Qs > Qd → surplus; traded quantity falls to Qd; possible government purchase of the surplus; producer income effects.

(b) For: raises price of the cheapest strong drinks → targets heavy/young drinkers (Scotland's minimum unit pricing). Against: inelastic demand for addicts; substitution and cross-border/illegal purchases; regressive; unlike a tax, the higher margin goes to retailers, not the government. Judgement with elasticity at the centre.

**Essay 2 (a)** Explain how a buffer stock scheme attempts to stabilise the price of an agricultural commodity. **[8]** **(b)** Discuss whether buffer stock schemes are likely to succeed in raising farmers' living standards. **[12]**
▸ Show mark guidance

(a) Price band; buy & store at the floor (supports price), sell stores at the ceiling (caps price); diagram with target band and shifting harvests.

(b) For: smooths volatile incomes → planning and investment become possible. Against: storage costs and perishability; band set too high → permanent surpluses drain funds (tin/coffee failures); doesn't raise the average price, only stabilises it; benefits may go to large producers. Judgement: stabilisation ≠ enrichment; often needs complementary policies.
